speaker: interviewer
text: And this is roll number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now Mark, you were telling us about the Nikolaev labor camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is in Nikolaev. Also they took us by flat truck. On top of the truck was about 20 kids. A German ambulan-- a German ambulance came across section and bumped into the truck, turned the truck over. And-- and we were laying on the truck, the wheels were up. And there was civilians-- even soldiers came running, because they had to save some people of the ambulance, and from the truck. They turned the truck back over. So the soldier, the German soldier, the chauffeur was not a scratch. The 12 kids-- the 12 boys-- their brains were spread on the pavement, and only about six survived. All the brains-- they were dead. They schlepped us in a hospital-- civilian hospital. And then there came an SS in a leather jacket, and he said, because we could move-- this hand, my left hand is still crooked, because the truck, the case of the truck was laying over my left hand. It never [INAUDIBLE]. That's what it is. And he said, you dirty dogs, you don't belong here. Out of the hospital. So back out of the hospital, back to that labor camp. And then so they took you to a boat-- there was standing boats there. And they were start dismantling their railroads, because the Russian were on their way coming back. So start dismantling railroads.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to take the tress-- the tress-- what, it's a heavy wood. I think-- tress-- forgot what they call it. Trestles, yeah? And get it straight up into the boat, loaded. By it wasn't straight up. It was on a chicken-- like a chicken ladder. It was all frozen, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you had to hold this on your shoulder in one hand. It was very heavy. And hold it with the other hand, so you don't slip it in the water. Because to go it was very slippery. But a lot of kids, unfortunately, couldn't make it, and they fell in the water, and there where they stayed. And that's-- that was part of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the Romanian came and they said those who didn't feel so good-- because it was German and Romanians-- said they should go back to Mogilev. And they sent you back to Mogilev. I was among them. What they going to do with me? I couldn't-- my left hand was not functional. Went back. My mother was very happy to see me there. And sure enough, after that, somewhere around '44, the Russian partisans came. And then the Russian army moved in, and they had-- they gave you a choice. You could go to the labor camp or join the army. And I-- if they talk to me, labor camp? Where you think I came from? [INAUDIBLE] for the Russian labor camp. The rumors were already around that they were treating the Jews in the Russian labor camp like the-- like the-- almost like the German. So I said I volunteer. They said, what do you know? I said, I speak several language. Good. We'll put you to the headquarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- so Mogilev, we start, so to speak, march, and marched out from Mogilev to Berlin. Took us only a year and a half to march, but it was marching. And that was no forced labor. But I'm saying we went through Warsaw. The Warsaw ghetto was already burned out. Across the Vistula, across the [? Tig. ?] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What unit were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: The unit that was the headquarter unit for int-- special interrogation purposes of the-- of prisoners. You know, they took German prisoners of wars, or even Ukrainian who were Vlasovs. They were turncoats against their own country. They called it the Vlasovs. And those were to be interrogated and then sent backs-- back. But they still get us-- they gave us, naturally, guns. So you still had to also fight. So as we came before Berlin, you know, wherever you went, all the way from Mogilev.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of this unit? Who was the general that you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was the famous-- not unit, that was the-- in the Supreme command, was General Zhukov-- the famous Marshal Zhukov. He was the Russian commander at that time. I mean, he was. He came with us to Berlin. And so we came-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, let me just think where-- where you left, because you asked for the commander. OK, so we got into Berlin. And before Berlin, there were tanks on the Autobahn-- German Autobahn-- all stationed for the final attack on Berlin. But the German resisted, and they bombed and everything. So I was going because I always had to go with two or three more people. They want to see what-- how the front looks, so with two or three more guys to catch somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were between the tanks. The guys in the tanks were sitting and playing cards. Not the [INAUDIBLE], because they were bombing. But we-- we walked between the tanks. Nothing will happen. Yes, it happen. So it came a big bomb split, gave me a big hole here overnight, and I start bleeding. And nothing can help when you start bleeding, because it's night. Where you go? Where you try? Nothing. So you lay under the tank until it gets light. In the meantime, all your shirt or whatever it is gets like a piece of cardboard from the blood, naturally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, in the morning, comes a tank. Put you in the tank and you go to one of these tents-- improvised tents, like you see in the evening here, like in the movies. And this-- and the doctors have big beards, because all they do is operate. And I come in, and that bleeds. And he says, hey, you lucky guy. Look at this-- we just took off the hands. This guy lost the eyes. So you are still lucky. You know, there's still a big hole here, in my right arm. You could-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said-- he pops a needle into me, like from a little can, pops it out. It's a big thing. He said, you're lucky-- it hits you here. Naturally, if it would hit me here, I wouldn't tell you this story today, either, because those are the facts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so down in Berlin, and in Berlin-- when I came to Berlin, I think I had enough. I figured I rushed, I run, I catch prisoner. I've done my duty in the camps, and the whole spiel. I said, I've got enough. So I kissed them goodbye, and I turned over, went to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you receive any metals or citations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but-- well, what it is, there's all kind of medals which are probably grown now in Germany, on a big dump-- dump place. Because I didn't believe it was-- I received several med-- because we took, actually, into-- for specialty, because that was the main command. So we received medal for overtaking the city of Berlin. And I'm sure you watch some of the movies, and I always-- when I watch some of the movies, I always look at, somehow my face was maybe missing over there. Because as soon I talk to some people, I-- I ask, and I always say, you're Jewish. But the-- during the second-- after the war was over, you didn't ask if you're Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was one word-- amcho. Amcho is the Hebrew word. Amcho. That's a Hebrew word I could best estimate-- are you a landsman of mine? But Hebrew. That's Hebrew. That's not Jewish, not Russian. So the first, that was like a secret code. We say, amcho. You kissed, you embraced, and keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Berlin, the last attack of Berlin, in the bunkers-- you know, it was all over there. I still have picture even from Berlin. And then I joined United Nation rehabilitation, Berlin Tempelhof, where the United-- where the Air Force-- the United States Air Force. And I worked there for them in administration. Again, administration, interrogation, and a lot of interesting things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, consider that from somewhere end of '45 through '48, till the Russian blockade started to Berlin. Then I decide, OK. So we got fine recommendation over there, again from the commanding general in the American-- as a fine citizen, he commend me to-- to America, to the finest integrity, with all the fine morals. He gave me a big letter. And there was a train going across the Russian border-- American. And they stopped them, of course, the train. And they-- they check out things, so-- it doesn't matter. And we go and I went to Braunschweig. That is-- was English occupation of Germany. It was Germany, Braunschweig. And there where I met my wife, Doris, and I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about Doris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Doris. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I met her there. She was just about recuperating. She just came from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she just came from Switzerland because she had TB when she was liberated. She was in Auschwitz and Stutthof. She came from Lodz-- Lodz ghetto. Background was Breslau, because her maiden name was Friedrich. F-R-I-E-D-R-I-C-H. Then somehow they wound up in Lodz, Litzmannstadt, where she lost her parents. And then she has-- she had two sisters, brother-in-law, and children. They-- they-- they perished in Majdanek, famous Majdanek. And she had a younger brother who, supposedly-- supposedly be in-- in Mauthausen. [INAUDIBLE] those famous-- he was a young kid. He was only, I understand, about 13 years old in this. So possibly couldn't survive. I don't know. But when I went to the Holocaust museum, her picture is there of Doris. And still some search. My own children still search. Maybe there's an uncle survive, which means Doris's brother-- the youngest. Maybe he is. Yeah, so we got married in Braun-- in Braunschweig.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- 1949, in summer. And then right away we-- we went into-- we took a boat. The boat's name was General Holbrook. That was a boat that took us to the United States. I remember the boat's name. It was like a military boat. And we arrived there in Erie, Pennsylvania, to her aunt, who was the only sister survived from her mother's sisters, who came about 75 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to go to Erie, Pennsylvania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because there was Doris's aunt, who survived so she wanted-- her aunt and their children. So she said, now at least I have some cousins, so I want you to go to Erie, PA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where you wanted to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I want to go-- you see, as soon Israel became a state, they were looking for volunteer for the Haganah, or underground. So I signed up, and I was almost sworn in. That was in Braunschweig. Was ready to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Doris said, what kind of a life? We get married now, and you're going to go to Israel and getting killed. I said, that's all I have. Because at that time, I didn't know yet that my mother is alive somewhere behind the Iron Curtain. So I figure I have nobody left. So at least I can still do a good deed, and go fight for good cause, for the state of Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, like any other woman-- may she rest in peace, very fine lady-- talked me into coming to Erie, PA. So we came to Erie, PA. We had our-- our first child. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of your first child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well the first child, unfortunately, is not alive anymore because we lost her when she was two years old. Her name was Esther. I have, even, a picture of her. And that almost destroyed her totally. Luckily we had friends-- and I say friends, not relatives no more-- who packed us in and say, you go to New York. There is some kind of far aunt or cousin of Doris. Not-- I didn't have any. And she had, also, a friend, who survived from concentration camp. So we went. We stay about a week and then we came back. And 1-2-3-- I should mention that Doris got pregnant again, [INAUDIBLE]. But Doris was very receptive, and she-- she got pregnant while even in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we refuse. We decide we will not have a German doctor deliver our children, or have an-- by somebody born in Germany-- a German citizen. Under no circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was abortion, the first children. No German-- I want to say something dirty, excuse me-- will ever touch me, or anybody of my family. Not that time, not now, or ever. I make it very strong and I feel very strong about it. No matter who they are. So this is-- Then we had the children. We had Carolyn first, and then we had Renee. And thanks God, they grew up. They have their own children now. But I worked, in the meantime, in Erie too. Tell us about your grandchildren. What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. One is Carolyn, and one is Renee, and one is Daniel. [INAUDIBLE] Carolyn has two children, and Renee has one. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they're grown up, thanks God, and they're very cute. Not because I'm the grandfather, but I think they are-- they hopefully will bring something good and-- good things, and something will come out of this, hopefully, if-- if things will be look different in this country, without any-- I don't want to go into that. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, how have your religious beliefs changed from before the war and now?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the only different religious belief, before the war, as a [INAUDIBLE], I was introduced and I spoke, I think, before and so as a-- more or less under-- not more or less, really under Orthodox. Keeping all the holiday, be strict and really believe a Jew is a Jew, and that is-- like they say in tradition. And that's what's important. As I came to this country, it's-- I became-- I was still a little tradition-- the tradition, but then I became conservative. Because where I lived, in Erie, PA, there was only a choice-- conservative or reform. And of course, every Jew in my book is good, no matter what they believe. But I really believe very strong. But I was-- my feeling are more toward the conservative part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mark, what effect do you think your experiences during the war had on the way you lived your life and raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very simple effect, because before the war, I believed that you have to be proud of what you are, and who you represent, and never put up a camouflage, or changing name, or anything else to be Jewish. We all believed that before the war. And the war, and the circumstances, and the event made this belief even much stronger, that we have to be proud of what we are, that we have to defend ourselves on any which way, any step, and let them know that we are-- we should be very proud who we are-- and when I say who we are, I'm talking about all the Jews, not just my family-- and where we're coming from. Because we produced, personally-- and there is proof-- one of the finest doctors, scientists, intelligent the world has, or will ever have. So therefore, that's what I believe. That didn't change, it just make me stronger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have any dreams?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dreams? Not much dreams. Because I like to tell you, I feel it, whatever happened-- to me and anybody else in the world-- it's-- I'm not unique, and its unfortunately happened. But I also feel I don't occupy myself with this thing-- occupy my thinking in my day. Because if I would, I wouldn't come that far, and I couldn't concentrate. What I like to do and still like to see in my children and grandchildren. So there was no way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, thanks God, of gut, and my strong will, and my background-- the way I was brought up. That was my strongest medicine, I could say, for survival. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's why I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's 50 years now since the end of the war. What message would you like to give to your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is very, very simple. Uncomplicated. It is, preserve 100% your heritage. Let everybody know what you stand for-- stand for with pride and honor. And love everybody you just have, and is around you, and about you. That is very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And hopefully, you can bring this tradition and this habit to the rest of the people, no matter where they are, or in this country or other countries. It's the most important thing that they should know. And I wish I could put-- how could say? I could turn the clock back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- if it just for moments, to show them the way the family really brought honor and pride, and were surrounded by friends of all denominations, of all people. If I could just show it to my children and grandchildren, and all the rest of them, maybe I fulfilled a small part. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you did, too, Mark. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather, Israel Jacob Rosen, from my mother's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: This place, I suggest-- I think was in Vienna, Austria. The place of the photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: And date of the photo, I would venture to say, like, in the early 1900s. This is my grandmother from my mother's side, Sarah Rosen. The place also somewhere in Vienna. Date of photo, approximately the turn of the century. This is my father, Karl Bronstein. Place-- the photo was taken in the late '30s-- 1930s-- in Romania. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Approximately, as I said, in the late '30s. And I am here with my student cap and some uniform, already in high school. [INAUDIBLE] This is my mother, Anna Bronstein nee Rosen. The photo was taken in approximately 1954, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here-- here is a group of all my coworkers in Berlin Templehof, in 1946, '47. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all officer of UNRRA, United Nation Rehabilitation. That's where I was. Here is Doris Bronstein, Bronstein, nee Friedrich, my late wife. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She is-- this picture was taken in-- in Braunschweig, Germany, somewhere around 1948, I think. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Here I represent-- we represent the second generation survivor the Holocaust. My dear daughters and son in-laws, my lovely grandchildren. Starting from the rear, from left to right, is Denny Hirsch and Renee Hirsch, which is my daughter. Denny's my son-in-law. Starting again from right to left is the son of Denny and Renee Hirsch, Daniel Hirsch. Then in the middle, we have Carolyn Lorber and Ira Lorber. Carolyn's my daughter. Ira is my son-in-law. To the right of Ira is Hallie Lorber, and to the left is Alice Lorber. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was taken approximately in 1993, in Cleveland, Ohio.
